text: Non-rearfoot strike running is retrospectively associated with lower rates of mild, moderate, and severe repetitive stress injury compared to rearfoot strike running.
Running economy does not differ between habitual rearfoot strike and habitual non-rearfoot strike runners at slow (10.8-11.0 km/h), moderate (12.6-13.5 km/h), and fast (14.0-15.0 km/h) speeds.
Imposing a non-rearfoot strike pattern on habitual rearfoot strike runners reduces running economy in the immediate term at slow and moderate speeds.
specific_value: SMD -1.67 at 10.8 km/h, SMD -1.26 at 12.6 km/h

text: Non-rearfoot strike running is associated with lower average and peak vertical loading rates compared to rearfoot strike running.
Non-rearfoot strike running is associated with reduced patellofemoral joint stress compared to rearfoot strike running.
Non-rearfoot strike running is associated with greater peak internal ankle plantar flexor moment, shifting load from the knee to the ankle and calf complex.

Changing foot strike pattern from rearfoot to non-rearfoot cannot be recommended for uninjured rearfoot strike runners, given no evidence of improved running economy and an associated shift in loading to the ankle and plantarflexors.
Non-rearfoot strike running is associated with lower knee flexion range of motion compared to rearfoot strike running.
